---
title: "CHE 318 Lecture 27"
subtitle: "Introduction of Humidification Process"
author: "Dr. Tian Tian"
date: "2026-03-16"
format:
  html: {}
  revealjs:
    output-file: slides.html
  pdf:
    output-file: L27.pdf
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="html" unless-format="revealjs"}

::: {.callout-note}
- Slides đź‘‰  [Open presentationđź—’ď¸Ź](./slides.html)
- PDF version of course note  đź‘‰ [Open in pdf](./L27.pdf)
- Handwritten notes đź‘‰ [Open in pdf](./public/L27_annotated.pdf)
:::

:::


## Learning Outcomes {.center}

After today's lecture, you will be able to:

- Recall fundamental knowledge about humidity 
- Understand the different ways of expressing water contentin air
- Analyze the temperature dependency of humidity
- Understand the arise of enthalpy and latent heat in water-air system


---

title: "CHE 318 Lecture" subtitle: "Humidification Operations and Cooling Towers" author: "Dr. Tian Tian" format: html: {} revealjs: slide-number: true chalkboard: false

Humidification operations: cooling towers

Use air to cool liquid water

A small fraction of water evaporates into the gas

Cooling comes mainly from latent heat removal

Key idea:


\text{liquid water loses energy} \rightarrow \text{water evaporates} \rightarrow \text{air gains } H \text{ and enthalpy}


---

What a cooling tower does

Warm water enters from the top

Air enters from the bottom

Packing creates large gas-liquid contact area

Water leaves cooler

Air leaves warmer and more humid





---

Humidity

Humidity $H$:

kg of water vapor per kg dry air


For water-air systems,


H=\frac{M_A p_A}{M_B(P_T-p_A)}

For air-water,


H = 0.622\frac{p_A}{P_T-p_A}

$p_A$: partial pressure of water vapor

$P_T$: total pressure



---

Saturation humidity and related definitions

Saturation humidity $H_s$:

humidity when gas is saturated with water vapor



H_s = 0.622\frac{p_{\mathrm{vap}}}{P_T-p_{\mathrm{vap}}}

Percent humidity:


H_p = 100\left(\frac{H}{H_s}\right)

Relative humidity:


H_R = 100\left(\frac{p_A}{p_{\mathrm{vap}}}\right)

$H_p \ne H_R$

They are equal only approximately at low humidity



---

Dew point

Dew point:

temperature at which the vapor in air just becomes saturated


At dew point,


p_A = p_{\mathrm{vap}}(T_{\mathrm{dew}})

Lower dew point means drier air

Dew point comes directly from vapor pressure data



---

Humid heat

Humid heat $c_s$:

heat required to raise $1$ kg dry air plus its associated vapor by $1$ K


For air-water,


c_s = c_B + Hc_A

c_s = 1.005 + 1.88H

Units:

kJ/(kg dry air K)




---

Heat required for a humid gas stream

For a stream containing dry air and water vapor,


Q = w_B c_s \Delta T

$w_B$:

mass flow rate of dry air


This is sensible heating of the gas-vapor mixture



---

Humid volume

Humid volume $v_H$:

volume occupied by $1$ kg dry air plus its associated water vapor



At 1 atm:

v_H = \frac{22.41}{273}\left(\frac{1}{28.97}+\frac{H}{18.02}\right)T

At general pressure:

v_H = \left(\frac{1}{28.97}+\frac{H}{18.02}\right)\frac{22.41}{273}T\left(\frac{1.013\times 10^5}{P_T}\right)

$T$ in K



---

Enthalpy of humid air

Humid air enthalpy includes:

sensible heat of dry air and vapor

latent heat carried by water vapor



General form:

H_g = c_s(T-T_0) + H\lambda_0

For air-water with $T_0=0^\circ\mathrm{C}$:

H_g = (1.005 + 1.88H)T + 2501.4H

Units:

kJ/kg dry air




---

Wet-bulb temperature

Wet-bulb temperature $T_w$:

quasi-equilibrium temperature reached by a small amount of water in adiabatic contact with air


Measured with a wetted surface or damp wick

Evaporation cools the wetted surface

Therefore,


T_w \le T_{\mathrm{dry\ bulb}}

Drier air gives a larger wet-bulb depression



---

Why wet-bulb temperature matters

Cooling towers approach the wet-bulb temperature of the entering air

Wet-bulb temperature is the practical lower limit for water cooling

If air is already humid, evaporation is weaker

If air is dry, evaporation is stronger



---

Mass transfer at the wet surface

Vapor flux from wet surface to bulk gas:


N_A = k_y (y_w - y)

$y_w$:

interfacial vapor composition at the wet surface


$y$:

bulk gas vapor composition


For dilute water vapor in air,


y \propto H

So the flux can be written in humidity form as well.


---

Coupled heat and mass transfer near the wet surface

Heat transferred from gas to the wet surface:


q = h(T-T_w)A

This heat supplies evaporation at the surface:


q = M_A\lambda_w(N_A A)

Combining heat and mass transfer gives


q = M_B k_y \lambda_w (H_w - H)A

This leads to the psychrometric ratio:


\frac{H-H_w}{T-T_w} = -\frac{h}{M_B k_y \lambda_w}


---

Physical meaning of the psychrometric ratio

Compares heat-transfer ability to mass-transfer ability

Links temperature difference to humidity difference

Explains why wet-bulb lines appear on a psychrometric chart

Important for humidification and cooling tower analysis



---

Reading the psychrometric chart

A psychrometric chart relates:

dry-bulb temperature

humidity

relative humidity

wet-bulb temperature

enthalpy


It lets us determine gas state without repeated property calculations

Common workflow:

locate $T$

locate humidity or relative humidity

read $T_w$, $H_g$, and other properties






---

Cooling tower picture: process intuition

Warm water contacts unsaturated air

Some water evaporates

Remaining liquid cools

Air leaves with:

higher humidity

higher enthalpy

often higher temperature


Main driving force for evaporation:


p_{\mathrm{vap}}|_{T_w} - p_A


---

Key operating notes for cooling towers

Water cannot be cooled below the entering-air wet-bulb temperature

In practice,


T_{\mathrm{water,out}} \gtrsim T_w

Air-side resistance usually controls the cooling rate

Contact area is critical

Packing strongly improves performance

Air flow may be natural draft or forced draft



---

Important design insights

Cooling is not just sensible heat transfer

Evaporation is the dominant mechanism

The gas phase is usually the limiting phase

Lower inlet-air humidity improves tower performance

More interfacial area increases cooling capacity

Wet-bulb temperature is the key reference temperature



---

What to remember

$H$ measures water vapor per kg dry air

$H_s$, $H_p$, $H_R$, and dew point describe gas moisture state

$c_s$, $v_H$, and $H_g$ are useful humid-air properties

$T_w$ is central to humidification and cooling tower analysis

Cooling towers work by coupled heat and mass transfer

The lowest achievable water temperature is controlled by air wet-bulb temperature

## Cheatsheet for packed bed design

![Physical copies distributed in class](../L25/public/cheatsheet-2phase.svg)



## Recall: packed tower height equation (individual phase)

General equation for gas. What the meanings for each term?

```{=tex}
\begin{align}
Z
&=
\int_{y_2}^{y_1}
\frac{V'}{k_y'aS}
\cdot
\frac{(1-y)_{im}}{(1-y)^2(y-y_{i})}
\, dy \\
&= \int_{y_2}^{y_1}
\frac{V}{k_y'aS}
\cdot
\frac{(1-y)_{im}}{(1-y)(y-y_{i})}
\, dy
\end{align}
```

## Recall: height equation for diluted system

If $x<0.1$ and $y<0.1$, typically use (for gas phase):

$$
Z = \left[ \frac{V}{k'_y a S} \frac{(1 - y)_{im}}{1 - y} \right] \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y_i}
$$

or even simpler

$$
Z = \left[ \frac{V}{k'_y a S} \right] \int_{y_2}^{y_1} \frac{dy}{y - y_i}
$$

what insights can we get?

## Motivation: transfer units for packed beds

From unit analysis, the term $\frac{V}{k'_y a S}$ characterizes the
height. We often use the concept of **transfer unit** to determine the
performance of mass transfer in each phase. For gas phase, we have the
"height" of the transfer unit $H_G$ as

```{=tex}
\begin{align}
H_G = \frac{V}{k_y' a S}
\end{align}
```

- $H_G$ is controlled by ratio of flow rate and mass transfer in gas phase
- smaller $H_G$ means more efficient packing (thus needing shorter tower)

## Define the number of transfer units

The rest of the equation for $Z$ can be rewritten as "number of
transfer units", similar to theoertical number of trays in a tray
tower. For gas phase general case, we have

```{=tex}
\begin{align}
N_G
=
\int_{y_2}^{y_1}
\frac{(1-y)_{im}}{(1-y)(y-y_i)}
\,dy
\end{align}
```

For dilute system, we can approximate $N_G$ by

```{=tex}
\begin{align}
N_G &= \int_{y_2}^{y_1}
\frac{dy}{y-y_i} \\
&= \frac{y_1 - y_2}{(y - y_i)_m}
\end{align}
```

## Transfer unit view of packed bed

For dilute system, we can see that

```{=tex}
\begin{align}
Z &= H_G N_G \\
  &= H_G \frac{y_1 - y_2}{(y - y_i)_m}
\end{align}
```

- Total height $Z$ is a stack of "transfer units" with height $H_G$
- Number of the units is governed by the total absorption $(y_1 - y_2)$ and driving force $(y - y_i)_m$
- Need to absorb more (larger $(y_1 - y_2)$)? đź‘‰ higher tower
- Operation line close to equilibrium? đź‘‰ higher tower

## Example 1: packed bed height estimation using transfer units

Similar example in [Lecture 24](../L24), Acetone is being absorbed by
water in a packed bed column having a cross sectional area of 0.186
m$^2$ at 293 K and 1 atm. The inlet air contains 2.6 mol% acetone and
outlet contains 0.5%. The gas flow is 13.65 kg mol inert air per
hour. The pure water inlet flow is 45.36 kg mol water per hour. The
coefficient $k_y' a$ is estimated to be $3.78 \times 10^{-2}$ kg
mol/(sÂ·m$^3$) and $k_x' a$ is $6.16 \times 10^{-2}$ kg
mol/(sÂ·m$^3$). The equilibrium line can be approximated by $y = 1.186 x$.

a) Now calculate the height using the dilute regime equation
b) Now calculate the height using $H_G$ and $N_G$ statements

## Example 1 diagram

![](./public/ex1-solution.png)

## Example 1 solution

Key numbers during calculation:

1. Liquid outlet $x_1 = 0.00648$
2. $(1 - y)_{im} \approx (1 - x)_{im} \approx 1$ đź‘‰ use dilute regime is justified
3. $(y - y_i)_{m} = 0.00602$
4. Average $V = (V_1 + V_2)/2 =3.852 \times 10^{-3}$ kg mol/s

Final results:

1. $Z=1.911$ m
2. $H_G=0.548$ m, $N_G=3.488$

## Practical estimation of $k_yâ€™ a$ or $H_G$

In real packed beds, coefficients are often obtained from correlations (using dimensionless numbers etc), which may depend on:


- $N_{Re}$, $N_{Sc}$
- Weight flow rates $G_y$, $G_x$
- Packing type (shape? diameter?)

![](./public/packed-geom.png)

## Design of packed bed in realistic situations

In many industrial applications, engineers are either interested in
one of the approach to estimate the desired tower height:

1. Estimate the correlation of $k_y' a$ and $k_x' a$ as functions of $G_x$ and $G_y$

$$
k_y'a = f_y(G_x, G_y, \text{pack type});\quad k_y'a = f_x(G_x, G_y, \text{pack type})
$$

2. Estimate $H_G$ (and / or $H_L$) from the correlation

$$
H_G = f_G(G_x, G_y, N_{Sc}, \text{pack type})
$$

Either way, the flow rates are locally dependent, so integration should be used. Let's a detailed  example from textbook.

## Example 2: concentrated packed bed design

(Example 10.7-1) A tower packed with 25.3 mm ceramic rings is used to
absorb SO$_2$ from aire using pure water at 293 K and 1 atm. The
entering gas contains 20 mol% SO$_2$ and leaving gas has 2 mol%. The
inert air flow is $6.53\times10^{-4}$ kg mol/s and inlet water flow is
$4.20\times10^{-2}$ kg mol/s. Cross sectional area of the tower is
0.0929 m$^2$. From literature, the





## Summary

In this lecture we have seen how the height of a realistic packed bed
tower is calculated and how to obtain relative statements using the
transfer unit concepts.

In next weeks, we will talk about the final topic: mass transfer
coupled with heat transfer. One useful example will be the
humidification tower.
